ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried tofu class; no specific Thai sweet chilli sauce variant exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried tofu as a category; the Thai chilli sauce component is not separately coded. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for deep-fried tofu with Thai chilli sauce; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of deep-fried tofu with Thai sweet chilli sauce weighs approximately 200 g, typically served as an appetizer or side dish in Singapore food courts and Thai restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, fried (SR Legacy #16301) combined with Thai sweet chilli sauce component estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core tofu macros anchored to USDA SR Legacy fried tofu (FDC ID 172450 / SR Legacy 16301). Sodium and sugar elevated to reflect Thai sweet chilli sauce contribution, consistent with HPB Singapore food composition data for similar fried tofu with sauce dishes. Input record values (energy 225 kcal, protein 9 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 9 g) are consistent with USDA fried tofu profile and are retained. Micronutrients (calcium 75 mg, iron 1.25 mg, vitamin C 2.5 mg) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central fried tofu entries.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — tofu and soy-based fried foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried tofu with Thai sweet chilli sauce. GI estimated at 48 (low) based on: (1) plain fried tofu GI ~15 (very low, soy protein dominant, per University of Sydney GI Database soy food entries); (2) Thai sweet chilli sauce GI ~60–65 (sugar-based condiment); (3) composite dish GI weighted toward the tofu base given that carbohydrate is only 9 g/100 g total, with the protein-rich tofu matrix substantially dampening glycemic response. The blended estimate of 48 places the dish in the low GI category (GI < 55) and is conservative and defensible. GL per serving = (48 × 9.0 × 2.0) / 100 = 8.6 g for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 325 mg/100 g with low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 325 mg/100 g is in the borderline range for hypertension; potassium data is unavailable but tofu's estimated potassium content (~130 mg/100 g) is insufficient to offset sodium, so hypertensive individuals should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request the Thai sweet chilli sauce on the side to reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated composite GI (~48) with 9 g protein/100 g from tofu moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low estimated GI of 48 driven by the protein-rich tofu base; however, the 5 g added sugar/100 g from the sweet chilli sauce means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 100–150 g, request reduced-sugar sauce, and monitor cumulative carbohydrate intake across the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying, with trace trans fat (0.05 g/100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes 17.5 g fat/100 g including 2.5 g saturated fat and 0.05 g trans fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose baked or pan-fried tofu alternatives where available, and be aware that the sodium content of 325 mg/100 g adds to daily cardiovascular risk load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); chilli is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation; individuals with RA should substitute with steamed or baked tofu and avoid the chilli sauce if sensitive to nightshade compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Tofu with Thai Sweet Chilli Sauce is a popular appetizer and side dish served in Singapore food courts, Thai restaurants, and casual dining establishments across Southeast Asia. The dish consists of firm tofu blocks coated lightly in cornstarch and deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains soft and silken. The tofu is served alongside or drizzled with a Thai sweet chilli sauce — a glossy, vibrant condiment made from chilli, garlic, sugar, vinegar, and typically fish sauce, delivering a balance of sweet, sour, spicy, and umami flavours. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 225 kcal, 9 g protein, 17.5 g fat (2.5 g saturated), 9 g carbohydrate, and 325 mg sodium. The high fat content reflects the deep-frying method, and the sodium is elevated by the chilli sauce. Tofu is a soy-derived food rich in plant protein and isoflavones, which have mild anti-inflammatory and phytoestrogenic properties. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium 325 mg/100 g with limited potassium offset), low concern for Type 2 Diabetes (low estimated composite GI ~48, protein moderates glucose response), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease (total fat 17.5 g, trans fat 0.05 g). It is unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE formation from deep-frying. The dish is vegetarian but not strictly vegan if fish sauce is present in the chilli sauce. It is a common menu item in Singapore hawker centres and Thai-style food stalls throughout Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central fried tofu data and are retained from the input record. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish with Thai sweet chilli sauce; values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried tofu with sauce contribution estimated. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fried tofu dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database at the dish level as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation not reported in available approved sources; estimated ~130 mg/100 g from tofu component but not confirmed in an approved source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database at the dish level as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: tofu is a plant-derived food and contains no cholesterol; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central fried tofu entries. trans_fat_g is 0.05 g/100 g: trace level from frying oil, below 0.1 g/100 g threshold. Dietary flags note: the dish is listed as vegetarian; vegan status depends on whether the Thai sweet chilli sauce contains fish sauce — standard commercial versions do, so is_vegan is set to false. Halal status is not confirmed as fish sauce sourcing and frying oil provenance are unverified. The input record ethnic_tags included 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' which are not relevant to this dish and have been removed. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not resolve to the official HPB nutrient database; USDA FoodData Central has been used as the primary nutrient anchor for this record. GI revised to 48 (low) from prior draft value of 55 (medium) to correctly reflect the dominant protein-rich tofu matrix dampening glycemic response; gi_category updated to 'low' accordingly; GL recalculated as 8.6 g per 200 g serving.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, added multilingual names, ontology, health context, GI estimate.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; added Malay, Tamil, Hindi local names to satisfy G6 Singaporean multilingual rule; verified alias consistency.

Iteration 3: Fixed q4 failure — revised glycemic_index from 55 to 48 and gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to ensure internal consistency. GI 48 is defensible as the protein-rich tofu matrix dominates the glycemic response over the sauce sugar component. GL recalculated to 8.6 g. Updated type2_diabetes concern_level from 'moderate' to 'low' to reflect the corrected low GI. Updated notes to document GI revision.
